provenance.title: Quantum Horizon: An evaluation of quantum computing as a threat to Bitcoin and Ethereum
provenance.abstract: Quantum computing threatens Bitcoin/Ethereum signatures (Shor) but not proof-of-work mining (Grover: only quadratic, sqrt(K) parallelization wall). A Monte-Carlo forecast blending hardware scaling, falling resource needs, fault-tolerance lag, and expert surveys gives a bimodal CRQC arrival: ~1-in-6 by 2035, ~30% by 2040, ~60% by 2050. Exposure is concentrated (~2.3M BTC irreducible) and mostly migratable; timely migration beats an optimistic 2035 machine.
key_claims: A systemic Monte-Carlo forecast blending hardware scaling, falling resource requirements, fault-tolerance readiness lag, and expert surveys yields a bimodal cryptographically-relevant-quantum-computer (CRQC) arrival distribution: about 1-in-6 by 2035, near 30% by 2040, about 60% by 2050, across an 80% range of roughly 2032-2060

Grover's algorithm poses no meaningful threat to Bitcoin proof-of-work mining: its speedup is only quadratic (sqrt(K) parallelization wall vs linear ASIC scaling), each iteration carries a large fault-tolerant per-operation cost (474,168 T-gates for a double-SHA-256 oracle), and difficulty adjustment absorbs any residual edge

Of Bitcoin's ~6 million quantum-exposed coins (~30% of supply), only ~2.3 million (~12%) are irreducibly at risk (dormant/lost/Satoshi-era); ~3.7 million are migratable, and ~65-70% of supply is protected-until-spent in fresh hash-based addresses

Ethereum's at-rest exposure is broader (50-65% of ETH sits at used accounts, most defensibly 55-60%) but more readily migratable via account abstraction (EIP-7702/EIP-8141), with a dormant-and-exposed slice of only about 0.1%

A migration-race model (Mosca's inequality) shows a prompt 2026 migration start finishes around 2029-2031, beating even an optimistic 2035 CRQC with years to spare, making governance speed -- not technology -- the binding constraint on a good outcome

architecture.primary_variant: A suite of six small, independently-calibrated quantitative models (systemic break-year Monte-Carlo forecast, mining-competitiveness model, Bitcoin exposure model, mempool-race model, Ethereum exposure model, migration-race/Mosca's-inequality model), plus a qualitative top-20 cryptocurrency quantum-readiness survey -- not a single trained system or neural architecture

modules.name: Systemic break-year Monte-Carlo forecast
modules.input_shape: swept scalar hyperparameters: hardware-doubling time (1.0-2.5 yr), resource-requirement halving time (4-20 yr), fault-tolerance readiness lag (2-12 yr), survey-vs-physics blend weight (0.25-0.75, default 0.5)
modules.output_shape: a bimodal probability distribution over CRQC arrival year, reported as cumulative probabilities at 2035/2040/2050 and an 80% credible range
modules.notes: Two independent sub-estimators (a bottom-up physics/hardware-scaling model, mode ~2052; an expert-survey model, mode ~2038-2040) are blended into one distribution. The exact Monte-Carlo sampling procedure (parametric distributional form for each swept range -- uniform, log-uniform, triangular -- and the number of draws) is not specified in the paper text; only the swept ranges and headline output percentiles are given.

modules.name: Mining-competitiveness model
modules.input_shape: Bitcoin June-2026 network difficulty (implying ~2^79 hashes/block), quantum gate speed (swept 0.0667-100 GHz), calibration benchmark (13.8 GH/s at 66.7 MHz, Aggarwal et al. 2017)
modules.output_shape: effective quantum 'hashrate' (TH/s) as a function of gate speed, compared against one ASIC (~200 TH/s) and the 2026 network (~860 EH/s); Figure 1

parameters.parallelization_scaling: sqrt(K) for K quantum machines, vs linear K for classical ASIC farms

modules.notes: The linear hashrate-vs-gate-speed scaling law is inferable and numerically consistent (13.8 GH/s @ 66.7MHz scaled linearly to 100GHz gives ~20.7 TH/s, matching the paper's reported ~21 TH/s), but is not written out as an explicit formula in the text -- it is reconstructed here from the calibration point and the reported output point.

modules.name: Bitcoin exposure model
modules.input_shape: three independent 2025-26 on-chain measurements (Glassnode 6.04M/30.2%, Coinbase ~6.9M, CoinDesk ~7M) plus Deloitte's 2020 scan (~25%), broken down by script type (P2PK/P2MS, P2PKH/P2WPKH, P2SH/P2WSH, P2TR)
modules.output_shape: three-way decomposition of exposed supply: irreducible (~2.3M BTC, 12%), migratable (~3.7M BTC, 19%), protected (~65-70%); Figure 3
modules.notes: The categorical split (irreducible vs migratable vs protected) is clearly defined qualitatively (dormant-across-all-script-types vs exposed-but-spendable vs never-yet-revealed), but the exact per-script-type accounting method used to arrive at the specific 2.3M/3.7M figures from the three raw on-chain measurements is not given as a reproducible formula in the text.

modules.name: Mempool-race model
modules.input_shape: fast-clock CRQC key-derivation time (9-12 minutes), Bitcoin block confirmation time (~10 min target), network propagation assumptions
modules.output_shape: per-spend success probability of a fee-bump replacement attack: best-case corner ~41% (9-min derivation, zero propagation, single confirmation), realistic estimate ~30% (fast-clock CRQC only; impossible for slow-clock trapped-ion/neutral-atom machines)
modules.notes: The model's internal timing/queueing mechanics (how network-propagation delay and confirmation-count assumptions combine into a single success probability) are described only narratively; the paper states it 'reproduces the literature's 41% figure as a best-case corner' but does not give the underlying probability calculation.

modules.name: Ethereum exposure model
modules.input_shape: total ETH supply, staked fraction (~32%), Deloitte's 2021 on-chain scan (a full ledger scan summing balances of every address with >=1 outgoing transaction), Beacon-deposit-contract accounting
modules.output_shape: at-rest exposure estimate of 50-65% of ETH supply, most defensibly 55-60%, cross-checked by a top-down composition (total minus staked minus contract holdings) and a bottom-up on-chain component build (corrected for a Beacon-deposit-contract over-count that naively gave 21%)
modules.notes: The two cross-checking methods (top-down composition and bottom-up on-chain build) are described narratively with headline output ranges, but the exact correction applied to the Beacon-deposit-contract over-count (from a naive 21% to the corrected 45-55%) is not given as a reproducible calculation.

modules.name: Migration-race model (Mosca's inequality)
modules.input_shape: migration start year (2026 prompt / 2030 delayed / 2033 severe-delay), migration duration (adoption-scenario dependent), CRQC arrival estimates (2035 aggressive, 2040 survey median, 2050 conservative)
modules.output_shape: migration-finish year per start-time scenario (2029-2031 prompt, ~2035 delayed, ~2038 severe-delay), compared against the CRQC arrival lines; Figure 4
modules.notes: Mosca's inequality itself is a standard, well-defined decision rule (time-to-start + time-to-migrate > time-to-CRQC implies risk); the paper's specific migration-duration assumptions per adoption scenario are swept but not enumerated in full in the visible text (only the resulting finish years per start-year scenario are given).

modules.name: Top-20 cryptocurrency quantum-readiness survey
modules.input_shape: signature scheme, exposure model (account-reuse vs UTXO/fresh-address vs shielded/stealth), and post-quantum migration program status, per coin, for the top 20 cryptocurrencies by market cap (mid-2026 CoinGecko snapshot)
modules.output_shape: an ordinal quantum-readiness rating from 1 (no post-quantum effort) to 5 (post-quantum live by default) per coin; Table 3, Figure 5

parameters.rating_5_definition: a post-quantum scheme is the live default

modules.notes: This is an analyst judgment rubric (sourced field assessment per the paper's own Appendix A, 'not a model'), not a quantitative/reproducible calculation; the precise scoring criteria that separate e.g. a 3 from a 3.5 or 4 are illustrated by example but not given as a formal point-based rubric.

connections.tensor_name: CRQC arrival year estimates (2035/2040/2050 percentiles) feed the migration-race comparison lines
the 'protected' (fresh-address) subset of Bitcoin's exposure decomposition is the population at risk during the transition window the mempool-race model quantifies
none -- these are explicitly independent per Appendix A's cross-validation rule (a model is credited with confirming another only if it shares no inputs/assumptions/method)
Ethereum's at-rest exposed fraction and account-abstraction migration path feed the Ethereum-specific migration-race reading

differences.scope: UTXO/script-type exposure model, mining-competitiveness model, mempool-race model, BIP-360/BIP-361 migration path
account-model exposure model, BLS12-381 consensus-layer exposure, KZG data-availability exposure, account-abstraction migration path (EIP-7702/EIP-8141)
qualitative readiness rubric applied across 19-20 cryptocurrencies beyond just Bitcoin and Ethereum, Section 6

mathematical_spec.name: Grover iteration count for Bitcoin proof-of-work attack
hyperparameters: Bitcoin June-2026 network difficulty (implying 2^79 classical hashes/block)

mathematical_spec.name: Effective quantum hashrate vs. gate speed (linear scaling from calibration benchmark)
mathematical_spec.latex: H_{eff}(f) = H_{benchmark} \cdot \frac{f}{f_{benchmark}}, \qquad H_{benchmark}=13.8\text{ GH/s},\ f_{benchmark}=66.7\text{ MHz}
hyperparameters: f (swept gate speed, up to 100 GHz)

mathematical_spec.name: Grover parallelization wall (sqrt(K) speedup vs linear classical scaling)
mathematical_spec.latex: H_{eff}(K \text{ machines}) = H_{eff}(1) \cdot \sqrt{K} \quad\text{(quantum)} \qquad\text{vs}\qquad H_{eff}(K) = K \cdot H_{eff}(1) \quad\text{(classical ASIC farm)}

mathematical_spec.name: Fault-tolerant SHA-2/3 preimage attack cost (Amy et al. 2016, cited)
Systemic break-year forecast (two-estimator probabilistic blend)
mathematical_spec.latex: P_{combined}(t) = w \cdot P_{survey}(t) + (1-w) \cdot P_{physics}(t), \qquad w \in [0.25, 0.75],\ w_{default}=0.5
undefined_symbols: P_survey(t) and P_physics(t) exact functional/distributional forms not given

mathematical_spec.name: Physics-based bottom-up hardware/resource-requirement crossing model
mathematical_spec.latex: Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/d_h}, \quad R(t) = R_0 \cdot 2^{-(t-t_0)/d_r}, \quad t_{CRQC,naive} = \{t : Q(t) \ge R(t)\}, \quad t_{CRQC} = t_{CRQC,naive} + L
undefined_symbols: Q_0, R_0, t_0 (initial calibration values not given numerically)

mathematical_spec.name: Mempool-sniping success probability
mathematical_spec.latex: P_{snipe,bestcase} \approx 0.41 \quad\text{(9-min derivation, zero propagation, single confirmation)}; \qquad P_{snipe,realistic} \approx 0.30 \quad\text{(fast-clock CRQC only)}
undefined_symbols: underlying probability model connecting derivation time, propagation delay, and confirmation count is not given

mathematical_spec.name: Ethereum at-rest exposure cross-check (top-down vs bottom-up)
undefined_symbols: exact Contract_fraction value not given numerically

tensor_semantics.name: Monte-Carlo CRQC break-year samples
tensor_semantics.shape_notation: [N_draws] scalar year values (N_draws not specified numerically in the paper)
tensor_semantics.dtype: float64 (or int, if binned by year)

tensor_semantics.name: Effective hashrate curve (Figure 1)
tensor_semantics.shape_notation: [N_gate_speeds] pairs of (gate_speed_GHz, effective_hashrate_THs), swept from ~0.0667 to 100 GHz

tensor_semantics.name: Bitcoin exposure decomposition vector
tensor_semantics.shape_notation: [3] categorical breakdown: [irreducible_BTC, migratable_BTC, protected_BTC], values in millions of BTC (2.3, 3.7, 13.4 per Figure 3)

tensor_semantics.name: Top-20 quantum-readiness rating vector
tensor_semantics.shape_notation: [19-20] ordinal ratings in {1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 3.5, 4}, one per surveyed cryptocurrency (Table 3, Figure 5)

training_pipeline.curriculum: Not applicable: this paper contains no trained model of any kind. Its quantitative content is a suite of Monte-Carlo simulations, deterministic calibrated formulas, and cross-source reconciliation estimates -- there is no gradient-based optimisation, loss function, or learned parameter anywhere. The closest analogue to 'hyperparameters' are the swept Monte-Carlo input ranges documented in architecture.modules (hardware doubling time, resource-requirement halving time, fault-tolerance readiness lag, survey-physics blend weight), which are simulation inputs, not trained/fit values.

datasets.name: Ethereum Foundation post-quantum team dormant-exposed-supply estimate (2026)
Roetteler et al. 2017 ECC resource estimate (arXiv:1706.06752)
Babbush, Gidney, et al. 2026 Google/Ethereum Foundation/Stanford whitepaper (arXiv:2603.28846)
Webber et al. 2022 conservative resource estimate (arXiv:2108.12371)
Global Risk Institute / Mosca-Piani Quantum Threat Timeline Report (2024, 2025 editions)

metrics: physical/logical qubit requirement ranges to break secp256k1/BLS12-381
cumulative CRQC arrival probability at 2035/2040/2050, and 80% credible range
Bitcoin exposure breakdown (irreducible / migratable / protected, in BTC and % of supply)
Ethereum at-rest exposure fraction (top-down vs bottom-up cross-check)
effective quantum mining hashrate vs. single ASIC and full network hashrate
migration-finish year under prompt/delayed/severe-delay start scenarios
ordinal quantum-readiness rating (1-5) per top-20 cryptocurrency

reported_results.metric: Logical qubits required to break secp256k1 (2026 frontier estimate)
Physical qubits required to break secp256k1 (2026 frontier estimate, upper bound)
Bitcoin exposed at rest (fraction of supply)
Ethereum at-rest exposure, most defensible range midpoint
Single quantum machine effective hashrate at 100 GHz (TH/s)

baselines: Roetteler et al. 2017 ECC resource estimate (2,330 logical qubits, 2.3x10^11 Toffoli gates)
Webber et al. 2022 conservative fault-tolerant hardware estimate (~317 million physical qubits)
Deloitte Netherlands 2020 Bitcoin exposure scan (~25% of supply)
Deloitte Netherlands 2021 Ethereum exposure scan (foundational ~65% at-rest measurement)
Global Risk Institute / Mosca-Piani expert-survey Quantum Threat Timeline Reports (2024, 2025)
evaluation_protocol.compute: Not specified. This is a Monte-Carlo simulation and cross-source quantitative-reconciliation paper with no machine-learning training; the described models (a handful of scalar Monte-Carlo draws over swept parameter ranges, deterministic calibrated formulas, and on-chain data reconciliation) are almost certainly lightweight enough to run on a standard laptop CPU in seconds to minutes, though the paper gives no explicit runtime or hardware information.
special_conditions: The combined break-year forecast is explicitly bimodal (a near-term survey-driven hump and a later physics-driven hump); its single median (~2046-2047) falls in the low-probability trough between the modes and the paper explicitly warns against reading the median as a best estimate
The survey-vs-physics blend weight is swept 0.25-0.75 as a sensitivity check: by-2035 probability ranges from ~8% to ~24% depending on this weight alone

Appendix A's cross-validation rule: 'a model is credited with confirming another only insofar as it shares no inputs, assumptions, or method with it' -- i.e. the paper's sub-models are designed to be independent checks on each other, not a single pipeline
The market survey of Section 6 is explicitly stated to be 'a sourced field assessment, not a model'

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The Monte-Carlo sampling procedure's exact distributional form for each swept parameter (hardware doubling time, resource-requirement halving time, fault-tolerance readiness lag, survey-physics blend weight) is not specified -- uniform, log-uniform, and triangular distributions over the stated ranges are all consistent with the reported output percentiles, and the number of Monte-Carlo draws is not given.
implementation_assumptions.basis: absence of an explicit distributional specification in the visible paper text
alternatives: uniform over each stated range (simplest, assumed default if reimplementing)
triangular with mode at a stated central estimate

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The physics-based bottom-up estimator's initial calibration values (Q_0, R_0, t_0 in the doubling/halving-time equations) are not given numerically in the visible text; they must be back-solved from the reported 2026 hardware state (~1,000-1,200 physical qubits) and 2026 resource requirement (1,200-2,330 logical qubits / 0.5-320 million physical qubits) if reimplementing.
implementation_assumptions.basis: the paper gives boundary conditions (2026 hardware and requirement state) but not the calibration constants in closed form
alternatives: calibrate directly to the stated 2026 values as t_0=2026
calibrate to an earlier historical hardware/requirement data point if available in the cited references

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The mempool-race model's internal probability calculation (how derivation time, network-propagation delay, and confirmation count combine into the reported 41% best-case and 30% realistic success probabilities) is described only narratively; the exact queueing/timing model is not given.
implementation_assumptions.basis: absence of an explicit formula in the visible text beyond the named scenario assumptions
alternatives: a simple race-condition probability based on the ratio of derivation time to block-interval time
a more detailed model incorporating mempool fee-market dynamics and miner selection behavior

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The Bitcoin exposure model's exact reconciliation method for combining the three raw on-chain measurements (Glassnode, Coinbase, CoinDesk) and the Deloitte 2020 baseline into the specific 2.3M/3.7M irreducible/migratable split is not given as a reproducible calculation -- only the qualitative category definitions and final headline numbers are stated.
implementation_assumptions.basis: absence of an explicit reconciliation formula in the visible text
alternatives: a simple average of the three measurements, then a separate dormancy-analysis pass to split irreducible vs migratable
a weighted average favoring the most recent/highest-quality source

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The top-20 quantum-readiness rating rubric (exact point criteria distinguishing e.g. a 2.5 from a 3, or a 3.5 from a 4) is illustrated by example in Table 3 but not given as a formal scoring formula; the paper itself describes this as 'a sourced field assessment, not a model.'
implementation_assumptions.basis: explicit statement in Appendix A that this is not a model
alternatives: treat as pure analyst judgment, not reproducible without the authors' full source-by-source assessment notes

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The BLS12-381 physical/logical qubit resource estimate (~1,950 logical qubits) is explicitly stated by the paper itself to be an interpolation, since 'BLS12-381 has no direct published resource estimate.'
alternatives: a direct future resource-estimate paper for BLS12-381, if published, would supersede this interpolation

ambiguities.location: Section 3.2 and Appendix A/B (Systemic break-year Monte-Carlo forecast)
ambiguities.description: The exact Monte-Carlo procedure -- distributional form per swept parameter, number of draws, and how the two sub-estimator distributions (survey and physics) are combined at the sample level (e.g. drawing from each with probability w vs. w-weighted density mixing) -- is described only at the level of swept ranges and headline output percentiles, not as fully reproducible pseudocode or a closed-form density.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: uniform sampling over each stated range, with the two sub-estimator samples mixed as a weighted density (P_combined = w*P_survey + (1-w)*P_physics)
alternatives: log-uniform sampling for the doubling/halving-time parameters

sample-level mixture (draw from survey model with probability w, else from physics model) rather than density-level mixture -- these produce different combined distributions even with the same marginal percentiles

ambiguities.description: The specific timing/queueing calculation connecting a 9-12 minute key-derivation window, network propagation delay, and confirmation-count assumptions to the reported 41% best-case and 30% realistic success probabilities is not given as an explicit formula in the visible text.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: a simple race-condition model where success probability scales with how much of the ~10-minute block interval is consumed by derivation plus propagation, calibrated to reproduce the stated best-case 41% figure exactly
alternatives: a more detailed model incorporating fee-market/miner-selection dynamics for the replacement transaction

ambiguities.location: Section 4.2 (Bitcoin exposure model) and Section 5.1 (Ethereum exposure model)
ambiguities.description: Both exposure models reconcile multiple independent on-chain measurements into a single headline range/split, but the exact weighting or averaging procedure used to move from (e.g.) three independent Bitcoin measurements at 25-30.2% to the specific 2.3M/3.7M irreducible/migratable split, or from Ethereum's top-down (55-63%) and bottom-up (45-55%) estimates to the 'most defensibly 55-60%' figure, is not given as a reproducible calculation.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: a simple average or midpoint-of-overlap reconciliation between the independent estimates
alternatives: a source-quality-weighted average favoring the more recent or more directly-measured sources
